It so happens that I just returned from a week of skiing. The resort I visited, Big Sky, is renowned for its steep terrain. To dissuade unjustifiably confident tourists from maiming themselves, the resort has labeled several runs as triple black diamonds. One of the informal criteria for the designation is that if you fall you can die. 

Despite my love of skiing, I’m thankful that such severe outcomes are unlikely for even the most difficult crossword puzzles.

commentBody: I don't get Caitlin's reference to CONN as being a common word. I wonder if she's confusing CON (which does have a number of common meanings -- e.g.,  dupe, against, short for 'convict', etc.) and CONN which exists primarily as a proper name.

I only snow skied one time.  I was 20.  I was waiting for a lesson and had on beginner skies – metal, thick, huge.  I was several yards away from the lip of a bunny hill, and the ground I was waiting on ever so slightly tilted toward that hill, and I started slowly gliding toward its lip.  Not knowing how to stop, I just kept going, and the next thing I knew, I plunged down, keeping my balance.

The bottom was curved like the bottom of the letter U, and when I reached it, I kept going because, again, I didn’t know how to stop.  I went up, then down (backwards!), and up and down, like the bottom of a pendulum, in ever decreasing arcs,  like something out of a cartoon, until I finally stopped.  

This was some time ago, and I don’t remember why I wasn’t immediately hooked on the sport, as the whoosh down that hill was quite thrilling.  But my life moved elsewhere, and skiing never became part of it.

The memory of this experience, however, remains vivid as ever.

I had so much fun with this, despite never having skied in my life. The idea of strapping SLIPPERY things to your feet and then hurling yourself down a mountain has never appealed somehow! Therefore the signs were meaningless to me. The gimmick was clear at CHINCILLAS, though it took reading Wordplay to get that HILL marked each downturn. I had to go back to admire that aspect.

It solved a tad easier than the average Sunday for me, but the solve was a real pleasure, trying to find those downward slopes.

And now; nit #100. I know, I know, in the US it’s a bathroom. But. If you’re clueing it as a British term, we never, ever, call any room a bathroom that doesn’t actually contain, like, an actual bath. You can use; loo, LAV, toilet, cloakroom, Ladies, Gents, bog, water closet or lavatory (if you’re very posh) to name but a few. If you ask for the bathroom in any public establishment you’ll get a quizzical look. Just saying. Right, with that off my chest I’m off to poo pick the Alpaca’s latrines. Not a bath in sight.

First of all, the title gives much too much away.  It's an easy puzzle and there's no reason to make it so much easier.  But there were some steps I had to take at the outset to learn what was to be entered and how.

Step 1:  It's CHINCHILLA and there's a HILL to be entered.  Is the green thing a rebus square?  No, the green thing doesn't get filled at all, and HILL gets entered in the tiny little circles heading downward.

Step 2:  Are all the tiny little circles filled by HILLs?  I check it at the short answer going straight down.  Yep, that's pHILLy.

Step 3.  Fill in all the HILLs.

Step 4:  Figure out why each HILL takes a different path down.  Aha -- green means an easy HILL; double diamond means a very hard HILL, and there's every level of skiing difficulty in between.

So now I've done all the heavy lifting, so to speak.  I've figured out the conceit -- and it's very cute.  To fill in the entire grid, which, frankly, isn't all that challenging would feel like an anticlimax.  Sort of like cleaning your skis and buffing up your ski boots after those exciting ski runs.  Moreover, there's a lovely MARCHING BANDS awaiting me in the Variety Puzzle which I know will require many more gray cells on my part.  So I'm stopping here -- having done what for me was the fun part.  I do think it's a cute idea for a puzzle.

CPT Phillips gave a talk at a local hobnob activity here back in '22. Two of the retired SEALs accompanied him, and put on a skydiving demonstration as the" Frogx Jumpers." (Just the SEALs, not the CPT)
While watching and listening to the CPT speak, I was amazed at all of his his adventures. I was further amazed at how accurately Tom Hanks portrayed him on the big screen. The gestures, vernacular, accent, all of it.
One of my favorite scenes in the movie was when the Navy PA tended to him after the rescue. 
Very moving scene. I asked the CPT about how he felt when she comforted him, he had no recollection of that ever happening. All he related was how professional and how well-poised the rescue team and medical staff was.
If you haven't read the book about his time leading up to and after the act of piracy, please do so. It's a great read.

I blame this on my traumatic memories of downhill skiing. As a risk averse and physically inept child I was forced to take downhill skiing lessons. Every weekend I would trudge to the hill, often in -20- -30 C weather (4- -22 F), to hurtle down the hill, feeling no sense of control. Never a broken bone, but I hated it. I think my brain simply refused to connect the dots when I looked through the puzzle.

Despite the PTSD, now understanding the puzzle, I admire it. Well done.

As an avid downHILL skier, I loved the theme! So clever to have the hills advance in difficulty/slope in accordance to the symbol. I see what you did there 👀!

Anyhow, I enjoyed doing it and thought the theme was very clever. Still, I would have liked some laughs and more tussle.

Double diamonds always remind me of a wonderful story -- at least in the end.
A 12-year-old in our church was hit by a car just before Christmas, many years ago.  At first he wasn't going to live, then never walk again....  On our annual youth ski trip at the end of January that year we stopped by the Rehab Hospital on the way home to all wish him well.

The following year on the same annual ski trip I was going up the ski lift that followed the double-black-diamond trail.  I looked down, and there was this same young man.  Skiing just had he had in previous years!  

He's now married with a family and only has a slight limp.  Miracles happen!

prone to perpetrate the fallacy of equating threat with capability
—C. S. Gray
b
: erroneous character : erroneousness
The fallacy of their ideas about medicine soon became apparent.
2
a
: deceptive appearance : deception
b
obsolete : guile, trickery
3
: an often plausible argument using false or invalid inference

All three meanings of "fallacy" are distinct from SLIPPERYSLOPE.

Can a SLIPPERYSLOPE result from one or more fallacies?  Sure.

But entirely non-fallacious ideas - correct assumptions - also and often lead to SLIPPERYSLOPEs.

Anti-vaxxers, for example, through insisting on the primacy of personal liberty, and despite fully understanding all the CORRECT and PROVEN principles of immunology, may well choose to lead civilization down the SLIPPERYSLOPE of re-emergence of preventable and highly morbid diseases.

The slipperiness of the slope has nothing to do with the truth or inaccuracy.

@RW. Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy “Fallacies”, considering them as “deceptively bad arguments” follows Copi in identifying 18 “core informal fallacies” https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/fallacies/ Number 18 is “The fallacy of the slippery slope”. The term does exist in the logician’s world. What makes this style of argument fail is not its formal structure but rather its application. The form of argument: “If A then B, if B then C… if Y then Z, but not Z, so not A”, or, as generally used as a rhetorical device “Z is undesirable so A is undesirable”, does not fail syllogistically. The fallacy arises in assuming the necessity, and relevance, of all intervening stages.

@Weak The "fallacy" clue isn't describing the term "slippery slope", it's referring to the "slippery slope fallacy", which is a logical fallacy whereby one argues that a starting condition will necessarily lead to a result, which will then necessarily lead to a further downstream result, etc., i.e. A leads to B, B leads to C, and C leads to D, therefore A leads to D.

This line of argument can be fine, but it is a fallacy when each step is not actually a guaranteed, likely, or even plausible outcome.

@miniMeissner - I'll just add to your fine explanation to say that it's not a true logical fallacy, but an informal fallacy. For this reason it is often referred to as the SLIPPERY SLOPE argument, rather than fallacy, which pleases the formal logicians of the world.
